quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Simple harmonic motion"?

options.text: A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: "Simple harmonic motion" is defined as: Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Angular frequency"?

options.text: ∫τ dt = ΔL; the angular analog of linear impulse equals the change in angular momentum
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
A frame moving with v_cm; simplifies collision analysis because total momentum is zero in this frame

quiz_questions.explanation: "Angular frequency" is defined as: ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Amplitude"?

options.text: A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley
A; the maximum displacement from equilibrium; determines the total energy E = ½kA²
F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it
U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: "Amplitude" is defined as: A; the maximum displacement from equilibrium; determines the total energy E = ½kA². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Phase constant (φ)"?

options.text: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different object; forces come in pairs
The initial phase angle that determines the starting position and velocity of the oscillation at t = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: "Phase constant (φ)" is defined as: The initial phase angle that determines the starting position and velocity of the oscillation at t = 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Velocity and acceleration in SHM"?

options.text: A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
v(t) = -Aω sin(ωt + φ), a(t) = -Aω² cos(ωt + φ) = -ω²x; acceleration is proportional and opposite to displacement
Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components

quiz_questions.explanation: "Velocity and acceleration in SHM" is defined as: v(t) = -Aω sin(ωt + φ), a(t) = -Aω² cos(ωt + φ) = -ω²x; acceleration is proportional and opposite to displacement. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude"?

options.text: Potential energy
Parallel axis theorem
Transverse vs. longitudinal
Simple harmonic motion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Simple harmonic motion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs"?

options.text: Center of mass reference frame
Angular frequency
Dot product
Simple pendulum period

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Angular frequency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A; the maximum displacement from equilibrium; determines the total energy E = ½kA²"?

options.text: Escape condition
Newton's First Law
Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Amplitude".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The initial phase angle that determines the starting position and velocity of the oscillation at t = 0"?

options.text: Energy dissipation
Rotational kinematic equations
Conservation of momentum
Phase constant (φ)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Phase constant (φ)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v(t) = -Aω sin(ωt + φ), a(t) = -Aω² cos(ωt + φ) = -ω²x; acceleration is proportional and opposite to displacement"?

options.text: Work by a spring
Impulse approximation
Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Center of mass reference frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Velocity and acceleration in SHM".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Simple Harmonic Motion is accurate?

options.text: Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
This principle is derived directly from Newton's Third Law—internal forces between objects cancel in pairs.
Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Simple Harmonic Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: The centripetal force is not a new type of force—it is the net radial component of real forces (tension, gravity, normal force, friction) that provides the inward acceleration needed to maintain circular motion.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Simple Harmonic Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Simple Harmonic Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).
The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Simple Harmonic Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).
Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Simple Harmonic Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Earthquake-resistant building design using tuned mass dampers (large SHM systems) to counteract the building's oscillation and reduce structural damage
Engineering design of crash barriers using the work-energy theorem to calculate the stopping force needed to safely decelerate vehicles of known mass and speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: Quartz crystal oscillators in watches using the precise SHM frequency of a quartz crystal (32,768 Hz) for accurate timekeeping
Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Simple Harmonic Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Simple Harmonic Motion?

options.text: Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Earthquake-resistant building design using tuned mass dampers (large SHM systems) to counteract the building's oscillation and reduce structural damage

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Simple Harmonic Motion in practice?

options.text: Door handle placement: handles are at the edge (maximum r) to maximize torque for a given applied force, making doors easy to open
Quartz crystal oscillators in watches using the precise SHM frequency of a quartz crystal (32,768 Hz) for accurate timekeeping
Analyzing bungee jump physics using conservation of energy between gravitational PE and elastic PE to determine maximum stretch and maximum speed
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Simple Harmonic Motion.
